# Small Requests

Small requests are where the system earns trust.

Add a paragraph. Clarify the opening. Make chapter three less abstract. Move the checklist to the appendix.

Each request should leave a trail that is visible enough to understand and small enough not to overwhelm the writer.

The seeded reader does not yet show change history, but the manuscript still needs to feel like something that could support that future.

Clear chapter boundaries and stable titles are part of that foundation.

The last chapter in this mock now carries more text because endings have their own layout behavior. A final chapter should not collapse into the backmatter. It should leave enough room for the reader to understand that the main sequence has ended and that the appendix is a different kind of material. If the final chapter is too short, that transition is barely visible.

Small requests are also the best way to test whether the system respects scope. A writer may ask for one extra paragraph in chapter ten, not a new book structure. They may ask for a photo in chapter four, not a redesign of the reader. They may ask for fewer chapters in the mock, not a change to the production workspace contract. The application should make those local edits feel natural.

The same discipline applies to fixture maintenance. A mock that grows without intention becomes noise. A mock that stays too thin becomes false confidence. This version aims for the middle: ten chapters, several longer bodies, and one image placed inside the flow of a chapter. It should be large enough to exercise scrolling and mixed content, but small enough to inspect by hand.

That hand inspection matters. A developer should be able to open the manifest and see the whole book order in a minute. They should be able to open a chapter and understand why it is long. They should be able to replace the image asset without learning a separate publishing workflow. The fixture should invite maintenance rather than becoming another hidden system.

The request is small, but it points at a larger habit: make the local mock honest. Give it enough content to reveal the reader's behavior. Keep it close to the real book workspace. Let it carry ordinary prose, because ordinary prose is what the interface must eventually serve.
